consultant.executiveSummary: Riverside Christian Academy projects $8,779,877 in Year 5 revenue with a 66.9% net margin. Margins are on the thinner side, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story.
consultant.biggestRisk: No major red flags detected, so continue refining assumptions as you gather real data.

recommendations.title: Strengthen Tuition Revenue Base
recommendations.description: Private schools typically derive 60–85% of revenue from tuition. At 16.2%, your tuition revenue share is lower than typical, so ensure your pricing reflects the full cost of education and is competitive for your market.

recommendations.title: Revenue Projection Jump from Prior Year
recommendations.description: Year 1 projects 64.7% revenue growth over last year's actual $2,400,000. Growth over 30% in a single year requires clear justification: enrollment surge, new funding stream, or tuition increase.

recommendations.title: Address Cash Reserve Deficit from Prior Year
recommendations.description: Last year ended with $120,000 in cash, only 0.7 months of expenses. Building reserves to 3+ months should be a priority. Consider a bridge line of credit while growing into net income.

consultant.lenderReadinessExplanation: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to net income, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

capTier.rationale: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.
capTier.source: Lending Lab Cycle 1 outcomes (Jan–Apr 2026, 18 packets, 3 lenders, 3 personas) — see ./lender-readiness-caps.calibration.md

cap.reason: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.

keyMetrics.name: Revenue per Student (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Healthy per-student revenue provides a solid foundation for sustainability.

keyMetrics.name: Cost per Student (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Total cost per student is $8,877, well within revenue per student of $19,759, leaving a healthy margin.

keyMetrics.name: Staffing Cost (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Staffing costs are well-controlled, giving you room for other priorities.

keyMetrics.name: Operating Cost (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Operating costs are lean relative to revenue.

keyMetrics.name: Net margin (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 1 shows a healthy surplus, a strong start for a new school.

keyMetrics.name: Net margin (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5 the model shows strong net income, and your school is on track for financial sustainability.

keyMetrics.name: 5-Year Revenue Growth
keyMetrics.interpretation: Strong projected revenue growth over the 5-year period.

keyMetrics.name: Capacity Utilization (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 5 enrollment approaches facility capacity, an efficient use of space.

keyMetrics.name: Days Cash on Hand (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Cash reserves cover 90+ days of expenses — a strong liquidity position.

keyMetrics.name: Cash Trough (Minimum Cash Month)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Your lowest cash point is Month 1 at $120,000. You have enough reserves to weather the collection gap — this is what lenders want to see.
keyMetrics.benchmark: Target: Positive cash balance every month

keyMetrics.name: Debt Service Coverage Ratio (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: DSCR is above 1.25x, and your operating income comfortably covers debt payments.

keyMetrics.name: Public Funding (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Public funding is a meaningful revenue stream without creating over-dependency.

keyMetrics.name: Operating Reserve (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5, the school has built a healthy operating reserve of 3+ months, a strong foundation for long-term stability.

enrollmentGuidance: Projected Year 1 enrollment (200) exceeds your documented pipeline of 170 students (170 retained) by 30. To make these projections achievable, build out your recruitment strategy for the remaining 30 seats: marketing plan, open house schedule, community partnerships, or referral programs.

topIssues.title: Let's extend your cash runway
topIssues.summary: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 17.5 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.
topIssues.whyItMatters: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
healthSignals.watchItem: Compare your real Year 1 enrollment against this projection as students enroll.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check this dimension after every enrollment or major staffing change in Steps 4–6.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain reserves as a buffer against seasonal revenue dips.
healthSignals.nextStep: Keep cash and reserves where they are; revisit Step 2: School Details after any large capital plan change.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: The school burns more cash than it generates in 5 months. Opening cash absorbs the gap today, but tight timing leaves no margin for late tuition or a delayed disbursement.
healthSignals.watchItem: Track receivables and payroll dates against the trough month so a single late payment doesn't tip the cumulative balance negative.
healthSignals.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue and tighten billing cadence (or grow an enrollment-driven line), or trim Step 7: Expenses, until monthly net stays positive.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Staffing at 32% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
healthSignals.watchItem: As you add staff, ensure staffing stays below 60% of revenue.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check after every staffing change in Step 6 to make sure payroll stays under 60% of revenue.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Facility costs at 1% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Watch for rent escalation clauses that could push this higher over time.
healthSignals.nextStep: Watch your lease for rent escalation clauses and re-check this signal in Step 7 each year.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: DSCR of 20.91x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain this ratio as you take on any additional debt.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check DSCR after any new debt is added in Step 5 to keep coverage at or above 1.25x.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue to pressure-test enrollment assumptions: recruitment pipeline, waitlist depth, retention rates, and collection reliability.
healthSignals.nextStep: Pressure-test your enrollment pipeline each cycle and re-check this signal after Step 4 updates.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 472 days of cash on hand exceeds the 90-day benchmark, providing a strong liquidity cushion.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain this buffer to weather seasonal revenue dips or unexpected expenses.
healthSignals.nextStep: Maintain this buffer; re-check after any large planned outflow.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 82.0 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue growing reserves toward 6 months for best-in-class financial health.
healthSignals.nextStep: Keep building toward 6 months of reserves through controlled spending in Step 7.

criteria.name: Leader Compensation
criteria.detail: School leader draws a salary. This is a fundamental requirement for sustainable operations.

criteria.name: Philanthropy Dependency
criteria.threshold: ≤25% in every year, decreasing over time
criteria.detail: Fundraising is healthy and encouraged. But your school must be able to keep the lights on without it. At full enrollment, your budget should balance on earned revenue alone. Grants should fund growth and mission extras, not basic operations.

criteria.name: Personnel Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Personnel costs exceed 60% of total expenses. This leaves too little room for facilities, curriculum, and contingency. A well-run school allocates no more than 60 cents of every dollar spent to people.

criteria.name: Facility Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Facility costs are well within the target range, leaving healthy budget room for personnel and programming.

criteria.name: Debt Service Coverage
criteria.detail: DSCR of 20.91x provides strong debt service coverage with healthy cushion across all years.

criteria.name: Current Ratio
criteria.detail: No accounts payable or current debt reported, so the current ratio cannot be evaluated. If your school has any bills due within 12 months, enter them under Opening Balances.

criteria.name: Net Margin Trajectory
criteria.detail: Net margin trajectory is healthy, reaching break-even by Year 3 and growing to a sustainable level.

criteria.name: Minimum Enrollment
criteria.detail: Year 1 enrollment of 200 students meets the minimum viability threshold.

criteria.name: Revenue Per Pupil
criteria.detail: Revenue per pupil of $19,759 meets the minimum threshold for sustainable operations.

lendingLabAssessment.summary: 1 area needs attention before applying. Most founders need 2–3 iterations to get here. Adjust your model and the assessment updates automatically.

assumptionFlags.currentValue: Y1 revenue per student of $19,759 is above the typical FL private range ($10,000–$18,000) but below the $20,000 ceiling.
assumptionFlags.benchmark: $10,000–$18,000 typical, $20,000 ceiling (internal estimate, calibration pending (FES-EO voucher schedule + FL private tuition surveys))
assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: Year 1 revenue per student computes to $19,759, above the typical $10,000–$18,000 band for FL private schools (internal estimate, calibration pending (FES-EO voucher schedule + FL private tuition surveys)) but below the $20,000 ceiling. Worth a one-line note for the lender on what positions you above the median.
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 1: Story and add a short pricing-position note explaining why $19,759/student is defensible for your FL private model.

assumptionFlags.currentValue: Tuition covers 160% of Year 1 total expenses
assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: Sticker tuition plus registration and activity fees fund 160% of your Year 1 operating cost base — a strong signal that the school can stand on tuition alone before philanthropy, vouchers, or grants. Tell the lender how you'll defend pricing and convert that capacity into collected revenue.
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue to confirm your collection rate and any scholarship discount, then capture your pricing-defense story in Step 1: Story so the lender packet leads with this strength.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -10%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 10% — the standard lender enrollment-miss sensitivity.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -20%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 20% — the stretch downside lenders use to confirm the school can survive a soft-launch.

scenarios.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
scenarios.description: Year-1 ESA, school-choice, and public-funding receipts reduced by 25% (≈ 3 months delayed) to simulate a slow first disbursement.

scenarios.name: Rent Shock +25%
scenarios.description: Occupancy & facility expense rows escalated by 25% — covers a lease renewal, market-rate reset, or unbudgeted CAM/utilities surprise.

scenarios.name: Founder Comp Normalized to Market
scenarios.description: Founder compensation re-cast at the market-rate benchmark (with benefits + payroll tax). The lender / board view of true operating cost.

narrative.headline: This model shows promise but has areas that should be strengthened before presenting to lenders.
narrative.summary: Riverside Christian Academy projects $8,779,877 in Year 5 revenue with a 66.9% net margin. Margins are on the thinner side, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story. 0 of 9 health dimensions are healthy, while 9 need attention. Cash runway extends 17.5 months before additional funding would be needed.

sections.title: Cover Page
sections.narrative: Financial model prepared for Riverside Christian Academy.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.narrative: Riverside Christian Academy projects $8,779,877 in Year 5 revenue with a 66.9% net margin. Margins are on the thinner side, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story.

linkedMetrics.label: Biggest Risk
linkedMetrics.value: No major red flags detected, so continue refining assumptions as you gather real data.

sections.title: School Overview
sections.narrative: Riverside Christian Academy is a Operating Private School organized as a 501(c)(3) Nonprofit with a Tuition-Based funding profile.

sections.title: Enrollment Plan
sections.narrative: Enrollment starts at 200 students in Year 1 and grows to 400 by Year 5 (100% growth).

sections.title: Revenue Model
sections.narrative: The revenue model is anchored to enrollment-driven income. Year 1 total revenue is projected at $4.0M, growing to $8.8M by Year 5.

rows.label: Residual reconciliation (collection rate, delinquency, scholarship treatment, other engine adjustments)

tables.title: Revenue Quality (Contracted vs. Projected vs. Donor / Policy Dependent)

rows.label: Hard revenue coverage of fixed + debt

insights.label: Risk framing — policy-dependent revenue
insights.body: 68% of Year 1 revenue comes from voucher / ESA / tax-credit scholarship funding (FL), which the model classifies as policy-dependent. That bucket is excluded from the hard-revenue numerator by design — it produces the conservative Hard Revenue Coverage (Y1) of 0.28× shown above. Per-pupil and voucher dollars ARE contractual once a child is enrolled and eligibility is verified; the conservative classification reflects legislative / court risk to the funding program itself, not to individual student revenue. Reviewers underwriting against enrollment-backed coverage should look at the per-program break-even and the enrollment-risk stress tests in addition to the hard-revenue ratio.

sections.title: Staffing Plan
sections.narrative:
Staffing costs are $1.3M in Year 1, representing 32.0% of revenue. This leaves healthy room for other operating costs. Founder compensation is normalized to market rate for the lender / board view. The 5-year normalization adjustment increases fully-loaded staffing cost by +$796,870 (salary + benefits + payroll tax). Year-1 DSCR moves from 22.02x to 21.01x and cash runway moves from 17.5 to 15.7 months when normalized. DSCR, runway, and net income figures elsewhere in this packet reflect the normalized view; the founder dashboard reflects the as-planned view.

Suggested founder compensation steps up year over year as projected enrollment crosses NAIS / NACSA size bands (escalated by COLA from Y1). Source: NAIS Head of School Compensation Report, 2023-24. Day-school median total cash compensation by total enrollment.. Size-band transitions during the forecast: Year 3 (150–300 students to 300–500 students, suggested rate $244,000). Each crossing bumps the lender-side market rate for the founder / head-of-school role.

tables.title: Suggested Founder Compensation Benchmark by Year
As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR

sections.title: Expense Summary
sections.narrative: Total expenses grow from $1.8M in Year 1 to $2.9M by Year 5.

sections.title: Capital & Debt
sections.narrative: The model includes 2 debt instruments totaling $1.4M.

sections.title: 5-Year Financial Projection
sections.narrative: The model reaches break-even in Year 1. Year 5 change in net assets is projected at $5.9M (66.9% margin).

linkedMetrics.label: Cash Trough (Minimum Cash Month)
linkedMetrics.benchmark: Target: Positive cash balance every month

insights.label: Breakeven enrollment
insights.body: Breakeven enrollment is 78 students (156% above Year 1 enrollment).

insights.label: Prior-year comparison
insights.body: Prior-year revenue was $2.4M; Year 1 projections represent a 64.7% change.

insights.label: Accounting basis
insights.body: All projections are prepared on an accrual basis; the school currently keeps books on a undetermined basis.

sections.title: Prior-Year Actuals
sections.narrative: Prior-year net income was $200K on revenue of $2.4M.

sections.title: Opening Balance Sheet
sections.narrative: Net position at model start: $-230K.

sections.title: Facility & Key Performance Indicators
sections.narrative: Breakeven enrollment is 78 students. Year 1 projects 200 students, providing a 156.4% cushion.

sections.title: Cash Flow Analysis
sections.narrative: Year-end cash covers 17.5 months of fixed costs. Opening balance: $970K total assets, $1.2M total liabilities, $-230K net position. Lowest cash month across the 5-year forecast: Year 1 — Jul at $477K.

sections.title: Debt Service Coverage
sections.narrative: Year-1 debt service coverage is 21.01x on the normalized (lender-primary) view. Minimum: 1.15x; target: 1.25x. See the "As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR" table for the founder / board (as-planned) view alongside this normalized series.

sections.title: Stress Testing
sections.narrative: All 6 stress scenarios maintain positive Year 5 net income, indicating financial resilience.

sections.title: Lender Readiness
sections.narrative: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to net income, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

sections.title: Key Risks
sections.narrative: 10 risk factors have been identified. Of these, 1 is rated critical and should be addressed prior to lender submission.

rows.label: Let's extend your cash runway
values: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.

Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening. Next step: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.

rows.label: Viability
values: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
Review revenue assumptions and cost structure to ensure operating margins support long-term sustainability.

rows.label: Liquidity
values: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.
Build operating reserves through fundraising, lines of credit, or phased expense deployment to maintain adequate cash position.

rows.label: Cash flow timing
values: The school burns more cash than it generates in 5 months. Opening cash absorbs the gap today, but tight timing leaves no margin for late tuition or a delayed disbursement.
Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

rows.label: Staffing burden
values: Staffing at 32% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
Evaluate staffing ratios against enrollment growth and consider phased hiring aligned to revenue milestones.

rows.label: Facility burden
values: Facility costs at 1% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.
Negotiate facility costs or explore co-location to keep occupancy below 15-20% of total revenue.

rows.label: Debt affordability
values: DSCR of 20.91x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.
Restructure debt terms, extend maturities, or increase operating income to improve Debt Service Coverage Ratio.

rows.label: Revenue concentration
values: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.
Strengthen enrollment pipeline evidence (waitlist, LOIs, retention data) and ensure revenue is anchored to demand-driven income rather than uncertain grants.

rows.label: Days cash
values: 472 days of cash on hand exceeds the 90-day benchmark, providing a strong liquidity cushion.
Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

rows.label: Reserve strength
values: 82.0 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
Establish a reserve fund policy targeting 3-6 months of operating expenses.

sections.title: Decision History
sections.narrative: No decisions have been tracked with an outcome yet. As the team marks saved scenarios as Pursued, Declined, or On hold, those outcomes (and any retrospective notes) will appear here so reviewers can see what actually happened versus what was modeled.

sections.title: Appendix: Key Assumptions
sections.narrative: Complete list of 26 assumptions used in this financial model, with each headline metric tied to its driving assumptions.

linkedAssumptions.value: Year 1 staffing cost: $1,265,734; Year 1 facility cost: $36,000; Year 1 operating expenses: $243,000; Year 1 debt service: $109,328; General cost inflation: 3.0%
linkedAssumptions.note: Driven by inputs in: Staffing, Expenses, Capital & Financing.

linkedAssumptions.label: Year 1 net income
linkedAssumptions.value: Year 1 enrollment: 200; Tuition per student: $12,500; Year 1 staffing cost: $1,265,734; Year 1 facility cost: $36,000; Year 1 operating expenses: $243,000
linkedAssumptions.note: Driven by inputs in: Enrollment, Revenue, Staffing, Expenses.

linkedAssumptions.label: Year 1 operating surplus
linkedAssumptions.value: Year 1 enrollment: 200; Tuition per student: $12,500; Year 1 staffing cost: $1,265,734; Year 1 facility cost: $36,000; Year 1 operating expenses: $243,000

riskMitigants.mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening. Next step: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Review revenue assumptions and cost structure to ensure operating margins support long-term sustainability.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Build operating reserves through fundraising, lines of credit, or phased expense deployment to maintain adequate cash position.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: The school burns more cash than it generates in 5 months. Opening cash absorbs the gap today, but tight timing leaves no margin for late tuition or a delayed disbursement.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Evaluate staffing ratios against enrollment growth and consider phased hiring aligned to revenue milestones.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Staffing at 32% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Negotiate facility costs or explore co-location to keep occupancy below 15-20% of total revenue.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Facility costs at 1% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Restructure debt terms, extend maturities, or increase operating income to improve Debt Service Coverage Ratio.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: DSCR of 20.91x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Strengthen enrollment pipeline evidence (waitlist, LOIs, retention data) and ensure revenue is anchored to demand-driven income rather than uncertain grants.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: 472 days of cash on hand exceeds the 90-day benchmark, providing a strong liquidity cushion.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Establish a reserve fund policy targeting 3-6 months of operating expenses.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: 82.0 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.

dscrSummary.trendDescription: Normalized (lender-primary) Y1 DSCR. See the “As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR” table for the founder / board (as-planned) view alongside the normalized series.

lenderReadiness.explanation: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to net income, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

healthDimensionCap.callout: Health Dimensions ratings capped at at_risk pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 25% evidence tagging, a healthy or watch-level signal cannot be trusted — the underlying inputs have not been anchored to evidence a lender or board can verify.

riskSeverityCap.callout: Risk Severity ratings raised to critical pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 25% evidence tagging, a 'low' or 'medium' risk band is not credible — the inputs that would otherwise prove the risk is contained have not been anchored to evidence. The severity is floored at 'critical' so the gap surfaces, not the false comfort.

flag.currentValue: Y1 revenue per student of $19,759 is above the typical FL private range ($10,000–$18,000) but below the $20,000 ceiling.
flag.benchmark: $10,000–$18,000 typical, $20,000 ceiling (internal estimate, calibration pending (FES-EO voucher schedule + FL private tuition surveys))
flag.defaultPrompt: Year 1 revenue per student computes to $19,759, above the typical $10,000–$18,000 band for FL private schools (internal estimate, calibration pending (FES-EO voucher schedule + FL private tuition surveys)) but below the $20,000 ceiling. Worth a one-line note for the lender on what positions you above the median.
flag.nextStep: Open Step 1: Story and add a short pricing-position note explaining why $19,759/student is defensible for your FL private model.

flag.currentValue: Tuition covers 160% of Year 1 total expenses
flag.defaultPrompt: Sticker tuition plus registration and activity fees fund 160% of your Year 1 operating cost base — a strong signal that the school can stand on tuition alone before philanthropy, vouchers, or grants. Tell the lender how you'll defend pricing and convert that capacity into collected revenue.
flag.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue to confirm your collection rate and any scholarship discount, then capture your pricing-defense story in Step 1: Story so the lender packet leads with this strength.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -10%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 10% — the standard lender enrollment-miss sensitivity.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -20%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 20% — the stretch downside lenders use to confirm the school can survive a soft-launch.

scenarios.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
scenarios.description: Year-1 ESA, school-choice, and public-funding receipts reduced by 25% (≈ 3 months delayed) to simulate a slow first disbursement.

scenarios.name: Rent Shock +25%
scenarios.description: Occupancy & facility expense rows escalated by 25% — covers a lease renewal, market-rate reset, or unbudgeted CAM/utilities surprise.

scenarios.name: Founder Comp Normalized to Market
scenarios.description: Founder compensation re-cast at the market-rate benchmark (with benefits + payroll tax). The lender / board view of true operating cost.

paragraphs: Riverside Christian Academy is a private school, based in FL. Riverside Christian Academy projects enrollment of 200 students in Year 1, growing to 400 by Year 5 against a stated capacity of 400 seats. Based on the canonical financial engine that powers this packet, the model reads as: Almost there - a few targeted edits will tighten the story. Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to net income, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

On the normalized base case (founder compensation marked to market), Year 1 debt service coverage is 21.01x. Operating cash runway extends 17.5 months from the open before additional capital would be required, with the tightest position at $2,296,259 of ending cash in Year 1. The model crosses operating break-even in Year 1, and Year 1 requires 78 students to cover fixed costs and debt service (20% of stated capacity).

Year 1 revenue mix is 17% contracted, 11% projected enrollment-driven, 68% policy-dependent, and 5% donor-dependent. The top risk surfaced is: Let's extend your cash runway (critical). Mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening..

Founder compensation has been normalized to market for underwriting. Across the modeled period the founder is drawing $796,870 below a market salary for the role. On a planned-comp basis the Year 1 DSCR would read 22.02x; on the normalized basis it lands at 21.01x, which is the figure used throughout this packet. The lender-primary view in this packet underwrites the school at the market cost of running it, not the founder's voluntary discount.

Under the toughest stress in the lender battery (Enrollment -20%), minimum DSCR holds at 16.76x and minimum ending cash at $1,843,518. Strong Year 5 net income Recommended next step before lender conversations: Address Cash Reserve Deficit from Prior Year.

topRisks.title: Let's extend your cash runway
topRisks.summary: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 17.5 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.
topRisks.mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.

highPriorityActions.title: Address Cash Reserve Deficit from Prior Year
highPriorityActions.description: Last year ended with $120,000 in cash, only 0.7 months of expenses. Building reserves to 3+ months should be a priority. Consider a bridge line of credit while growing into net income.
